episodes.theme: Preservation by accident — how the great collections of Alexandria and Baghdad survived not through intention but through dispersal, copying, and the selfishness of patrons who wanted credit for what they could not destroy

The hidden argument of access — tracing who was kept outside the Bodleian, the Escorial, and the Congressional Library, and why the open-access movement is the latest unresolved chapter of a dispute as old as the first locked scroll-room

turning_points.description: Hypatia reveals that the Mouseion's decline was not fire but fiscal — Ptolemaic subsidies quietly withdrawn as Rome tightened its grip, forcing scholars to carry texts outward rather than inward; the first migration-as-preservation

Ibn al-Nadim shows that al-Mamun's translation orders were driven by a horoscope predicting a Greek-speaking rival — knowledge weaponised by astrology, yet the translated texts outlived every political motive that funded them

Mele confronts Sor Juana with the Bodleian's 1877 admission of women — nearly three centuries after Sor Juana was silenced and her books confiscated — forcing the question of whether open access is progress or merely delayed repetition

Gottfried learns that Jefferson's sale of his library to rebuild Congress was a private profit on a public loss, collapsing the republican ideal of knowledge-for-all into the same patron-entanglement that defined every prior institution
